author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
title: Cyprian’s Testimonies Concerning God’s Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Chapter 25 [IX.]—Cyprian’s Testimonies Concerning God’s Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But now it plainly appears in what way Cyprian proclaims the grace of God against such as these, when he is arguing about the Lord’s Prayer.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For he says: “We say, ‘May Thy name be made holy,’ not that we wish for God that He may be made holy by our prayers, but that we beseech of Him that His name may be made holy in us.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But by whom is God made holy, since He Himself makes holy?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But, because He says, ‘Be ye holy, because I also am holy,’ we ask and entreat this, that we who were made holy in baptism may continue in that which we have begun to be.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And in another place in the same epistle he says: “We add also, and say, ‘Thy will be done in heaven, and in earth,’ not in order that God may do what He wills, but that we may be able to do what God wills.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For who resists God that He may not do what He wills?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But, since we are hindered by the devil from obeying God with our thought and deed in all things, we pray and ask that God’s will may be done in us.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And that it may be done in us, we have need of God’s will, that is, of His help and protection; since no one is strong in his own strength, but he is safe by the indulgence and mercy of God.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: In another place also: “Moreover, we ask that the will of God may be done both in heaven and in earth, each of which things pertains to the fulfilment of our safety and salvation.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For since we possess the body from the earth, and the spirit from heaven, we are ourselves earth and heaven; and in both, that is, both in body and in spirit, we pray that God’s will be done.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For between the flesh and the spirit there is a struggle, and there is a daily strife as they disagree one with the other; so that we cannot do the very things that we would, in that the spirit seeks heavenly and divine things, while the flesh lusts after earthly and temporal things.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And, therefore, we ask that, by the help and assistance of God, agreement may be made between these two natures; so that while the will of God is done both in the spirit and in the flesh, the soul which is newborn by Him may be preserved.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And this the Apostle Paul openly and manifestly declares by his words.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: ‘The flesh,’ says he, ‘lusteth against the spirit, and the spirit against the flesh; for these are contrary the one to the other, so that ye cannot do the things that ye would.’”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And a little after he says: “And it may be thus understood, most beloved brethren, that since the Lord commands and teaches us even to love our enemies, and to pray even for those who persecute us, we should ask even for those who are still earth, and have not yet begun to be heavenly, that even in respect of these God’s will may be done, which Christ accomplished in preserving and renewing humanity.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And again, in another place he says: “But we ask that this bread should be given to us daily, that we who are in Christ, and daily receive the Eucharist for the food of salvation, may not, by the interposition of some more heinous sin,—by being prevented, as those abstaining and not communicating, from partaking of the heavenly bread,—be separated from Christ’s body.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And a little afterwards, in the same treatise he says: “But when we ask that we may not come into temptation, we are reminded of our infirmity and weakness, while we so ask as that no one should insolently vaunt himself; that none should proudly and arrogantly assume anything to himself; that none should take to himself the glory either of confession or of suffering as his own, when the Lord Himself teaching humility said, ‘Watch and pray, that ye come not into temptation: the spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak;’ so that while a humble and submissive confession comes first, and all is attributed to God, whatever is sought for suppliantly, with fear and honour of God, may be granted by His own loving-kindness.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Moreover, in his treatise addressed to Quirinus, in respect to which work Pelagius wishes himself to appear as his imitator, he says in the Third Book “that we must boast in nothing, since nothing is our own.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And subjoining the divine testimonies to this proposition, he added among others that apostolic word with which especially the mouths of such as these must be closed: “For what hast thou, which thou hast not received?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But if thou hast received it, why boastest thou as if thou hadst not received it?”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Also in the epistle concerning Patience he says: “For we have this virtue in common with God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: From Him patience begins; from Him its glory and its dignity take their rise.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: The origin and greatness of patience proceed from God as its Author.”
